data.title: abrdn Asia-Pacific Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

The investment portfolio underperformed its benchmark by approximately 0.7%, with the overweight US dollar exposure the largest factor detracting value. The overweight exposure to the Indian local currency bond markets contributed positively, as the bond market outperformed the Asian and Emerging Market local currency bond markets. The portfolio’s overweight to Indonesia detracted value relative to the benchmark as investors’ concerns about ongoing fiscal prudence weighed on sentiment. Meanwhile, the lack of exposure to the better-performing non-Asian EM market, Poland, contributed to some underperformance relative to the benchmark. Although Asian credit spreads widened, the underweight exposure to HY sovereign bonds contributed positively to the relative performance.1

NAV return data includes investment management fees, custodial charges and administrative fees (such as Director and legal fees) and assumes the reinvestment of all distributions.

The Fund is subject to investment risk, including the possible loss of principal. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized.

Although the Fund’s NAV underperformed it benchmark, the NAV increased by 2.87%, benefitting from the positive contribution from the use of leverage. The strategic use of leverage is beneficial to the income generated by the fund due to the positive interest rate differential between the increased earned and the cost of leverage, however the mark-to-market impact of interest rate swaps used to hedge the cost of leverage was a detractor as interest rates fell during the period.

The investment portfolio performance is gross of fees and expenses and not subject to leverage. The NAV performance is net of fees and expenses and includes the impact of leverage. The benchmark performance is not subject to fees and expenses and is not subject to leverage. For current holdings information, please visit abrdn Asia-Pacific Income Fund, Inc. - Portfolio Holdings

Economic & market overview

The 3-month period ending April 30, 2025 was very eventful for global trade, geopolitical relationship and market performance. To say that the new US administration has begun its term in office with a great amount of gusto is an understatement. The impact that many Presidential executive orders culminating in the aggressive and widespread April 2 Liberation Day tariffs and then subsequent delays and watered-down implementation has had a dramatic impact on the performance of bond markets and begun to erode the market’s perception of US exceptionalism. Stagflation, volatility have emerged as often referenced terms in participants analysis. High yield markets globally and the US dollar bore the brunt of the market adjustments.

The US yield curve bull steepened over the period under review, with the 2-year yield falling by 60 basis points ('bps') while the yield on 10- and 30-year instruments declined by 33 and 11 bps, respectively. Yields largely declined in response to fears that US President Trump’s tariffs would negatively affect economic growth and lead to more aggressive easing by the US Federal Reserve (Fed). That said, markets were particularly volatile towards the end of this period, with the Bank of America ‘Move’ index - a measure of volatility in US Treasuries - rising to around 140, its highest level in over two years. Indeed, at the time of writing, bond yields had shot back up, particularly from the 10-year tenor onwards, as investors reacted to news that the US and China would lower tariffs on each other for the next 90 days. This volatility was largely due to the fact that the tariff spats had made it increasingly difficult to gauge the underlying economic picture, with businesses, consumers and investors all trying to predict or react to the latest iteration. The Brent crude oil price fell by almost 20% over the period under review to $61 a barrel, with the trade wars eroding the outlook for demand and Saudi Arabia signalling a move to increase supply.

Asian bonds

Asian bond markets performed strongly once again over the period under review, with 10-year yields falling across most countries in the region. Hong Kong (-49 bps) led the gains, reflecting the moves in the US as well as the relative attractiveness of carry trades, which are likely to ebb and flow depending on changing US rates expectations, with the risks for bonds and the Hong Kong dollar seemingly balanced. Singapore (-44 bps) and Thailand (-41 bps) also performed strongly, with investors reacting to Singapore’s trade dependency on the US and the Bank of Thailand’s decision to cut rates by 50 bps over the period. India (-34 bps) and South Korea (-29 bps) also posted solid returns, with the latter buoyed by news that FTSE Russell will include Korean Treasury Bonds in the World Government Bond Index from April 2026. Yields also fell in Malaysia (-15 bps) and Indonesia (-12 bps). Conversely, the Philippines (+4 bps) was the only market that saw its 10-year yield increase over the period under review.

China’s 10-year yield was flat over the period. The performance of this market was a fairly accurate representation of how investor sentiment evolved during the three-month period, with the yield on its 10-year bond spiking by as much as 30 bps. While the impact of its tariff wars with the US is hard to quantify, given their evolving nature, China’s export growth rate for the second quarter of 2025 will likely experience a significant decline, and the need for hedging with domestic demand policies has become more pressing. Investors are anticipating an earlier reduction in the reserve requirement ratio and interest-rate cuts.

Asian currencies

Having benefitted from the US exceptionalism theme for some time, the US dollar did strengthen in the immediate response to the 2 April tariff announcements. Subsequent policy restatements and deals have seen the unravelling of this exceptionalism theme, with the US dollar depreciating during April. Asian currencies have been the beneficiaries of capital reallocations away from the US in favour of the region. Most Asian currencies strengthened against the US dollar. The Philippine peso (+4.30%) and Singapore dollar (+3.72%) led the outperformance, while the Malaysian ringgit (+3.24%) also performed well. Elsewhere, the Indian rupee (+2.45%), South Korean won (+2.06%), Thai baht (+0.76%) and Hong Kong dollar (+0.47%) all strengthened. Conversely, the Indonesian rupiah (-1.85%) underperformed significantly, while China’s offshore yuan (-0.37%) also depreciated.

Asian credit

Asian credit performed well over the period as the JPMorgan Asian Diversified Credit Index returned 1.80% in US dollar terms, while the index spread widened by 21 bps. High-yield ('HY') debt (1.17%) underperformed investment grade ('IG') (+1.90%). Real estate (+7.71%) was the top-performing sector, while all other sectors were around +0-2% for the period, except for metals and mining (-0.71%). Hong Kong (+2.61%) and China (+2.14%) were the standout countries, with Sri Lanka (-6.38%) and Pakistan (-4.19%) the main laggards.

EM debt & currencies

Performance for emerging-market ('EM') debt was mixed during a historic three-month period for financial markets. The announcement of US reciprocal tariffs triggered a global sell-off, which was later mitigated by a 90-day pause in their implementation. Amid this turmoil, hard-currency EM bonds (+0.57%) posted positive returns over the period, led by IG (+2.23%), which was the standout performer over the three months, with HY detracting (-0.98%).

The Middle East was the standout region. That said, Bolivia (+6.12%) was the top performer due to expectations of a more market-friendly administration after this year’s election. Lebanon (+5.97%) continued its recent strong performance after the cabinet approved a draft law to restructure the country’s banking sector, which mostly aligns with the IMF’s conditions. Conversely, Senegal was the worst performer after S&P downgraded the country’s rating to B with a negative outlook, following weaker fiscal metrics revealed by auditors. This came after a downgrade by Moody’s in February, with the IMF confirming the auditors’ findings and estimating public debt at 105.7% of GDP. EM local-currency bonds underperformed both their hard- currency counterparts and Asian local-currency markets. The JPMorgan GBI-EM Global Diversified Index (unhedged in US dollar terms) returned +5.53%. Poland (+12.62%) was the top performer after annual inflation fell by more than expected to 44.4% and the central bank commenced its rate-cutting cycle, while Turkey (-14.69%) exhibited the largest underperformance in a difficult period for the country after the central bank raised the policy rate to 46% following the arrest of Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu, which triggered a sell-off in Turkish assets.

Outlook

The market has settled down from the peak of the tariff- driven panic. Optimists seem to be taking the Trump administration’s 90-day delay on the tariff implementation as capitulation and a reason to look past it. We are not quite so sanguine, as we feel it is likely that this extended period of uncertainty could weigh on economic growth, ignite inflation, or both. This may take more time to filter into the hard data than many expected. The good news is that if there is a slowdown, we expect it to be relatively short-lived and shallow.

Although the first round of tariff announcements most negatively impacts Asia, there is every likelihood that the outcomes for Asia will not be as bad as first feared. The reasons for this remain Asian economic fundamentals and policy-making credibility, the resilient intra-regional and trade linkages with the rest of the world, and, of course, plenty of opportunity for deals to be struck with the US. Additionally, over the course of the past couple of years, many multinational companies have diversified their supply chains geographically, so thinking about trade on a purely bilateral basis may provide a too simplistic assessment.

The events following “Liberation Day” prompted global investors from varying geographies and of varying types to reconsider exposures to US assets. In some cases, this reaction is considered retaliatory, others defensive, but the unpredictability of policy, the potential for significant fiscal slippage and/or the increased likelihood of stagflation are motivating capital allocators to revisit the opportunities outside the US. The period of “US exceptionalism” appears over, with the US dollar likely to continue to come under pressure. With a benign inflation outlook, Asian policymakers, amid improved capital flows, Asian policymakers have the capacity and willingness to ease policy to support domestic demand.

The Indian bond market has largely been supported by the commencement of the Reserve Bank of India (RBI)'s rate-cutting cycle alongside liquidity injections to support the market. In April, the RBI cut the policy rate by 25 bps to 6.0% and, more importantly, moved to an accommodative stance. India’s Monetary Policy Committee’s expectations for inflation to remain near target for the fiscal year, providing scope to support economic growth via further rate cuts. Current data, therefore, points to expectations for a deeper easing cycle to support India’s solid, if softening, economic growth.

On the other hand, Indonesia continues to suffer from investor concerns about the institutional credibility of the new administration, especially with respect to fiscal prudence.

Asian credit markets are also likely to be resilient in the face of any market volatility. Within Asia, the credit market does comprise a large proportion of sovereign, quasi-sovereign and state-owned enterprise bonds, with different credit considerations and much lower credit risk compared with corporates. Additionally, Asian corporate issuers tend to be the largest players in their home markets and thus not only benefit from decent domestic revenues but also access to local bank financing and now local currency bond markets. This materially reduces the earnings and liquidity risks in the near term. The sell-off experienced in April, in our view, is more technical in nature and not reflective of the fundamental strength of Asian issuers. Meanwhile, net supply for the sector is negative and a technical source of support for the sector. From a sectoral allocation, we focus on issuers servicing local consumption, where amid uncertainty, more spending is happening on home turf.

data.title: Delcath Systems: Growing Fast, But Business Model Is Fragile
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Delacth has experienced strong growth for its HEPZATO treatment and trades at an attractive valuation. However, it faces both current and future competitive pressures that will likely weigh down sentiment and valuation multiples.

Introduction

Delcath Systems (NASDAQ:DCTH) is a company focused on HEPZATO, a treatment for certain forms of metastatic uveal melanoma, which has spread to the liver. This is a "kit" which includes an injection and a filtration system, which allows for more saturation compared to other forms of treatment.

The particular indication that HEPZATO treats is quite narrow, with most patients having failed other treatments and then having no other approved options. Delcath estimates only about 800 Americans are eligible per year.

While the treatment does appear to be somewhat superior to existing options, unfortunately, it does not provide a cure. Delcath's studies show the median HEPZATO patient survives 20.53 months, which is 4-10 months longer than other treatments. It is notable, though, that survival rates were not clearly superior to tebentafusp, also known as Kimmtrak, which is made by competitor Immunocore (IMCR).

The treatment itself is highly complex, requiring special centers and three different medical professionals involved in administration.

In addition to HEPZATO, the company also has CHEMOSAT, a chemotherapy treatment, but this has only about 10% of sales of HEPZATO. Finally, the pipeline shows that Delcath is trying to expand the indication from liver cancer to other cancers, targeting a small population of patients that have failed multiple other treatments.

Huge Growth and High Prices

Despite the small patient population and complex treatment, sales so far have been growing rapidly, with strong sequential revenue growth, and analysts expect annual growth for 2025 to be roughly 155%.

How has Delcath managed to achieve such high numbers? In part, high prices. The price for HEPZATO is very high, at $187.5k per round of treatment, with the average patient undergoing 4 rounds of treatment, with 6-8 week intervals. This means that the average patient is paying $750k for a year's worth of treatment. I've done in-depth research of over 100 biotech companies, and outside of single-dose gene therapy treatments, I had never seen a treatment priced this high. Out of curiosity, while writing this article, I did attempt to look up other treatments (again, excluding gene therapies) for any disease that cost more and found four, including the aforementioned Kimmtrak. So despite the very high price for HEPZATO, it's not the most expensive treatment in the space.

Potential Future Competition

In addition to current competitors, such as Kimmtrak, HEPZATO could eventually face other competition, such as from Replimune (REPL). It has a unique mechanism of effectively training the body's own immune system to kill the cancer cells, meaning it is possible for it to treat cancer that has spread to other areas.

Another potential competitor is Ideaya Biosciences (IDYA), which is developing an oral therapy, even easier to administer than Replimune's IV treatment. It has the potential to be a first in line treatment for uveal melanoma, through a unique mechanism of blocking a specific protein, which causes cancer cells to stop reproducing and die.

Both of these treatments are still in clinical trials. However, Replimune and Ideaya's treatments could potentially have the following advantages over Delcath:

  • They would be much easier to administer, and thus likely much less expensive.
  • The unique mechanisms could prove much more effective.
  • They would have much broader indications, helping make the case for a new standard of care.

Given how far along these drugs are in clinical trials, I am going to guess that Delcath won't see a competitor emerge until 2027 at the earliest. However, more positive readouts from Replimune or Ideaya, or another potential competitor, could serve to dampen sentiment around the long-term opportunity for Delcath and contract their valuation multiple.

Financials

Delcath's financials are in decent shape. The company does not appear to have any debt. It recently turned profitable. It has patent protection until at least 2032. Stock based compensation appears to be at reasonable levels, costing less than 2% of market cap for 2024. The company is expanding coverage and is signing an expanded agreement with Medicaid.

Gross margins for the most recent quarter were 86%. The company recently turned profitable, with $0.03 per share in GAAP earnings, and management confirmed that cash flow will finance further research.

During the most recent earnings call, management was asked if they had plans to use free cash flow to buy diagnostic devices to pair with treatment. Management politely said no:

We always keep an open mind, and a number of things have come across our desks to look at, none of which seem like it makes sense. I think usually in licensing, it's usually along the lines of what else could the sales force sell in this type of business. I think taking our eye off this very profitable product to sell something that is high volume, low margin that takes a lot more sites to sell into doesn't make a lot of sense. Now if something comes along that is more a profile in terms of focused highly technical sale, that might make some sense, but nothing's come across to date.

I view this refusal to gamble money on new product lines as a positive, as I have seen many companies blow through cash on acquisitions that were overhyped and overvalued.

Valuation

In my opinion, price to sales ratio is the best way to value biotechs, since it allows for easier apples to apples comparisons and does not punish companies for engaging in research. The average biotech P/S ratio is 5.5.

Delcath's current P/S is 10.3, which looks expensive at first glance. However, given the fast rate of growth, this will not be an expensive stock for long. The consensus analyst estimate for 2026 revenues is $135 million. That means, given Delcatch's current $555 million dollar market cap, they are trading at 4.1 P/S ratio on a forward basis. This compares favorably not only to the average biotech, but also to other high growth biotechs that I've covered in the past. For example, TG Therapeutics (TGTX), is priced at roughly a 7.0 P/S ratio on a forward basis, and BridgeBio Pharma (BBIO) is priced at roughly a 10.8 P/S ratio on a forward basis.

Meanwhile, Delcath has said that their current and potential future indications target a billion-dollar-plus TAM.

If Delcath ended up with peak sales of a billion dollars and a 5.5 price to sales ratio, that would indicate a potential valuation of 5.5 billion, roughly ten times today's price. However, I'm not at all confident that Delcath's peak sales will be nearly this high, and by the time peak sales do come, I'm not confident that it will command a 5.5 P/S multiple.

As mentioned above, HEPZATO is both very expensive and difficult to administer. The treatment itself doesn't necessarily justify the high price, as it could be best described as life-prolonging, rather than life-saving or curative. This combination of less than stellar efficacy and very high cost means Delcath will be more sensitive than other therapies to disruption, which is why I view the potential threat from new competitors as very serious.

It's not clear that HEPZATO is superior to existing competition, such as Kimmtrak. While HEPZATO and Kimmtrak do not compete for the exact same pool of potential patients, Kimmtrak currently has roughly four times higher sales.

If we assume that HEPZATO will continue to grow and will eventually take half of this market at peak sales, or $0.5 billion, and that by the time it gets there competitive pressures will cut the P/S multiple to 2.5, that would take market cap to $1.25 billion, or roughly $37/share, which is more than double today's price.

What's worse is that I do not see things improving further from here. I believe that as time passes, investors excited about the next big thing in biotech will be attracted to other companies like Replimune or Ideaya, or perhaps another emerging competitor.

Risks

The primary risks to HEPZATO are competitive pressures, which could weigh on shares in three different ways.

First, HEPZATO is facing off against Kimmtrak, which is made by a larger and more well funded competitor, which also happens to be the market leader. HEPZATO carries the risk of slowing down, especially since it cannot claim to be completely superior to Kimmtrak.

Second, as next generation therapies like those from Replimume or Ideaya progress through trials, sentiment may sour on HEPZATO, both because it is less exciting and also because the estimation of long-term peak sales may decline.

Third, if competing therapies do finally hit the market, likely at some point after 2026, HEPZATO may not be able to compete because these new drugs may be cheaper, easier to administer, carry fewer side effects, and be more effective.

The Bottom Line

While Delcath might initially look expensive, the valuation is one of the most attractive parts of this potential investment, with significant upside even if we make several conservative assumptions. Still, I'm not entirely convinced. The business is in a fragile position within the competitive landscape, which does not inspire confidence or enthusiasm. I'm not going to take a position, but the strong growth profile, reasonable valuation, solid financials, and sensible management team at Delcath are good enough to merit a Hold rating.

data.title: IVV: U.S. Bellwethers In An Ultra-Low-Cost ETF Wrapper
data.description:
iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:IVV) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund offering ultra-low-cost exposure to U.S. bellwethers, or the 500 most generously valued and arguably the most capable companies that can be found in the U.S. equity universe.

Thanks to the simplicity and straightforwardness of its strategy, super low expense ratio, and performance that is infamously complicated to outmatch, IVV is something of a one-stop shop for U.S. equity investors with a wide set of goals, be it short-term gains or long-term wealth accumulation for retirement, etc.

Today, I would like to offer an in-depth analysis of this product that will cover the key aspects investors should consider before buying into it, including the details of the index creation process, performance it delivered in the past, composition of the portfolio, and risks this vehicle comes with.

IVV Basics

  • IVV was incepted on May 15, 2000.
  • In terms of categories, the ETF falls into the Large Blend sub-class within the U.S. Equity class.
  • BlackRock Fund Advisors is the investment adviser.
  • IVV tracks the S&P 500 index; S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC is the index provider.
  • IVV is one of the largest ETFs in the world, with assets under management of $591.28 billion as of June 7, 2025.
  • It is also one of the most liquid, with a 3-month average daily dollar volume of $4.42 billion.

What Does The iShares Core S&P 500 ETF Do?

According to the iShares website, IVV is managed passively, with the S&P 500 index being the cornerstone of its strategy.

However, an important remark here is that, as we know from the prospectus, BlackRock Fund Advisors "uses a representative sampling indexing strategy to manage the Fund," which essentially means that the overlap between the underlying index and the ETF's portfolio may be below 100%.

Established in 1957, the S&P 500 is sometimes equated with the U.S. market, and for good reason, as it captures a large group of the most valuable companies in the country.

As described by S&P Global:

The index measures the performance of the large-cap segment of the U.S. market. Considered to be a proxy of the U.S. equity market, the index is composed of 500 constituent companies.

Together with the S&P 400 and the S&P 600, with which it has by design zero overlap, this index is one of the components of the S&P Composite 1500.

What securities can join the index?

As we know from the methodology document available on the S&P Global website, the S&P Index committee decides which stocks will join the S&P 500 and which will go.

This bellwether index welcomes common stocks listed on a few eligible exchanges, including the NYSE, Nasdaq Global Market, etc. The company must be "U.S.-domiciled." At the same time, American Depositary Receipts, exchange-traded funds, limited partnerships, master limited partnerships, and a few other types of securities described in greater detail on page 7 of the methodology cannot compete for a place in the index.

For context, the Nasdaq-100 Index, which is tracked by the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ), welcomes ADRs, so there are a few such securities in the QQQ portfolio, with AstraZeneca PLC (AZN) being an example worth mentioning.

Next, a company must satisfy the size criterion, with a market cap of $20.5 billion being the bare minimum. It is worth explaining that though shrinking market cap is one of the main reasons for a company's removal from the S&P 500 index (i.e., as a consequence of its lackluster performance, Celanese (CE) was removed from the index in March 2025 and transferred to the S&P 600, which measures the performance of the small-cap echelon), it certainly does not immediately get expelled when its market cap drops below that milestone. For instance, as of June 8, 2025, 138 companies in the index (3.48% weight) had a market cap below $20.5 billion.

Candidates are also screened for liquidity and public float.

Besides, there is a quality-focused ingredient, which is called 'financial viability.' Simply put, it means that to become eligible for inclusion, a company must report positive GAAP net income over the most recent quarter, plus the sum of the net income figures for the most recent four quarters must be above zero as well.

This is one of the top differences between this index and, for instance, the Russell 1000, another barometer of the U.S. market, which is tracked by the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB). It is worth reminding here that despite its surging market cap, Tesla (TSLA) was loss-making for a long time, so it joined the S&P 500 only in December 2020. But as the Russell 1000 does not require positive net income, TSLA easily qualified for inclusion about a decade earlier, in September 2010, contributing to its gains.

Obviously, precisely like in the case of market cap, the 'financial viability' rule does not imply that upon turning loss-making, a company would immediately drop from the index and from the IVV portfolio.

To corroborate, as of June 8, 2025, 26 companies (1.82% weight) in the IVV portfolio had a negative trailing twelve months GAAP net income, with one example being Intel Corporation (INTC), with a 0.17% weight.

What weighting scheme is used?

The S&P 500 features the float-adjusted market cap-based weighting scheme. The main consequence of that is the most generously valued U.S. companies (i.e., the Magnificent Seven or the trillion-dollar league) climb to the very top of it.

Another evident consequence is that mid-cap names, if represented, have a microscopic weight in it and contribute just a few bps to its performance, if at all. For a fuller context, as of June 8, companies with a market cap below $10 billion accounted for just 0.38% of the IVV portfolio, as per my calculations. At the same time, mega-caps, which are valued at more than $100 billion, had a 73.2% weight, and the trillion-dollar cohort accounted for 34.2%.

The S&P 500 has a quarterly rebalancing schedule. However, adjustments tend to be minor, which is reflected in the ETF's turnover of just 3%.

For example, in the first five months of 2025, the index, and, hence, the IVV portfolio, have seen just five additions and five deletions (four stocks replaced in March in one in May). In June, no adjustments were made.

How Does IVV Fit In A Portfolio?

Thanks to IVV's time-tested, simple yet potent strategy, it should fit as a core holding in almost any portfolio focused on capital appreciation regardless of the timeframe an investor has set, be it a multi-decade portfolio strategy with a focus on a comfortable retirement or short-term gains.

Who Is IVV For?

IVV should work for investors seeking convenient, cheap exposure to the most valuable U.S. companies (or to the U.S. market as a whole), regardless of whether they use taxable or retirement accounts.

In other words, this product is for investors who, instead of beating the market, which is notoriously complicated even for industry juggernauts like hedge funds, would like to just benefit from its ups and wait through its downs anticipating that in the long term, even after a few steep declines, returns would be nothing short of robust.

And in fact, IVV's long-term performance (and so did the performance of the U.S. market) has been exceptional.

  • Over more than 25 years of its existence, IVV had just five calendar years with negative total returns, namely 2001, 2002, 2008, 2018, and 2022.
  • In 12 calendar years, its annual return exceeded 15%, chief among them being 2013 (32.29%).
  • $10,000 invested in IVV shortly after its inception grew to $65,161 as of the end of May 2025.

And even though its returns were less impressive compared to those of QQQ, it offered a much more comfortable risk profile. For instance, QQQ was much more volatile over the period, with a 22.6% standard deviation vs. IVV's 15.2%. And the Nasdaq 100 ETF had a maximum drawdown of 79.66% (during the dot-com bubble burst) vs. 50.79% of IVV (during the Great Recession).

A due remark here is that IVV is not a good choice for value investors. The problem is that as higher growth companies tend to climb to the key positions in the index, it is reasonable to expect IVV's valuations to be constantly inflated. For a fuller context, as of June 7, 2025, there were 95 stocks in this portfolio with a B- Seeking Alpha Quant Valuation grade or higher. But owing to the weighting, they accounted for just 8.5%, while those with a D- grade and weaker, including all main ten holdings except for BRK.B, had an 82.4% weight.

How Is IVV Built?

As of June 5, 2025, there were 507 holdings in the IVV portfolio. In the holdings dataset, 503 were marked as 'Equity' (99.7% weight), with others being cash, cash collateral & margins, and futures.

Owing to the weighting scheme of the S&P 500, a solid share of IVV's net assets are deployed to the ten key holdings (35.8%), with all of them being trillion-dollar companies, mostly from the information technology, consumer discretionary, and communication sectors that have been the locomotives of the U.S. market growth in the 2010s and into the 2020s.

This group (with a nuance being Alphabet, which, as in the case of QQQ, is represented by the Class C and Class A shares) is presented in the table below:

Ticker Weight Company Name Market Cap Sector
(MSFT) 6.88% Microsoft Corporation $3.496 trillion Information Technology
(NVDA) 6.79% NVIDIA Corporation $3.458 trillion Information Technology
(AAPL) 5.97% Apple Inc. $3.046 trillion Information Technology

(JPM) 1.45% JPMorgan Chase & Co. $738.489 billion Financials

Data from IVV and Seeking Alpha. Weights as of June 5, 2025. Market cap data as of June 7, 2025

At the same time, the bottom 100 stocks, including such well-known companies as Ralph Lauren (RL), Clorox (CLX), and Invesco (IVZ), account for just 2.1% and thus have a microscopic impact on IVV's returns, which vividly illustrates disproportions in allocations investors should pay attention to.

Regarding sectors, information technology dominates the portfolio, accounting for over 32%.

Information Technology 32.08%

Created by the author using data from IVV. Sector weights as of June 5, 2025

As we can see from IVV's sector composition as of May 12, 2014, which I obtained using the Wayback Machine, the role of IT in the S&P 500 has seen a momentous increase over the previous decade, while cyclical sectors like energy have seen their weights diminish.

Sector Weight
Information Technology 18.47%

Created by the author using data from iShares obtained using the Wayback Machine. Sector weights as of May 12, 2014. A nuance here is that the telecommunication services sector was replaced with communication services in September 2018 and thus no longer exists

One of the reasons for that was a prolonged period of ultra-low interest rates in the U.S. that provided solid support to tech companies, as their valuations reflect potential cash flows that they are yet to generate in the distant future, and interest rates directly impact them (i.e., they appear more valuable when rates go down and vice versa).

IVV's value, growth, and quality mix

Even though IVV is not supposed to represent any particular style (so it falls into the 'blend' category), what investors should expect to see under the hood regarding factors is small, if not diminutive, exposure to the value factor, almost perfect quality characteristics, and material exposure to growth. All these facts are the consequence of the S&P 500's weighting scheme that pushes the high achievers to the very top.

Metrics IVV
Market Cap $1,065 billion
EY 3.67%
Adjusted Earnings Yield 3.82%
P/S 8.44
EV/EBITDA 25.99
Adjusted EV/EBITDA 21.34

Adjusted ROE 20.38%
